bio.photo_notes: Watts Sherman Humphrey (1927-2010), "father of software quality," National Medal of Technology 2003

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Sarasota, Sarasota County, Florida per FamilySearch

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle and nephew: Treasury Secretary and software pioneer
cross_connections.notes: HD4679 George Magoffin Humphrey (Secretary of the Treasury) is the uncle of HD4695 Watts Sherman Humphrey (software engineering pioneer). Both sons of Caroline Mary Magoffin Humphrey's children — George through HD4678 × I00953 first-born son; Watts through HD4682 Watts Jr.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins: Army G-2 chief and software quality pioneer
cross_connections.notes: HD4700 GV Strong is the first cousin of I00957 Katherine Strong (HD4695 Watts Humphrey's mother), making GV Strong and Watts Humphrey first cousins once removed. Both achieved national prominence: one as WWII Army intelligence chief, one as father of software quality.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of software quality; National Medal of Technology
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Humphrey founded the Software Process Program at Carnegie Mellon's Software Engineering Institute in 1986, developing the five levels of process maturity that became the Capability Maturity Model — adopted by the Department of Defense as a mandatory standard for defense software contractors and now embedded in software development organizations worldwide — and in 2003 President Bush awarded him the National Medal of Technology, the highest U.S. honor for technological innovation, for what colleagues called making software engineering a discipline rather than an art. Earlier at IBM, where he had been a vice president, he led the team that introduced the first commercial software license in the late 1960s — a foundational act in the history of the software industry — and he accomplished all of this despite severe dyslexia, putting himself through the University of Chicago and Illinois Institute of Technology on physics degrees before pivoting to computing.

narrative_blocks.header: His uncle was Eisenhower's Secretary of the Treasury
narrative_blocks.body: His uncle George Magoffin Humphrey — his father's brother — served as the 55th Secretary of the Treasury under President Eisenhower from 1953 to 1957, his signature appearing on approximately 4.5 billion currency bills; and through his mother Katherine Alice Strong, whose father George Hooker Strong traced his line back to Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong, Watts Humphrey had a second independent Hooker-line descent from the same seventeenth-century Connecticut origins. His parents were third cousins — both great-great-grandchildren of Captain James Hooker of Windsor — making Watts Sherman Humphrey the software pioneer one of the most multiply-descended figures in the entire Hooker family tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of software quality; National Medal of Technology
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Humphrey founded the Software Process Program at Carnegie Mellon's Software Engineering Institute in 1986, developing the five levels of process maturity that became the Capability Maturity Model — adopted by the Department of Defense as a mandatory standard for defense software contractors and now embedded in software development organizations worldwide — and in 2003 President Bush awarded him the National Medal of Technology, the highest U.S. honor for technological innovation, for what colleagues called making software engineering a discipline rather than an art. Earlier at IBM, where he had been a vice president, he led the team that introduced the first commercial software license in the late 1960s — a foundational act in the history of the software industry — and he accomplished all of this despite severe dyslexia, putting himself through the University of Chicago and Illinois Institute of Technology on physics degrees before pivoting to computing.

narrative_blocks.header: His uncle was Eisenhower's Secretary of the Treasury
narrative_blocks.body: His uncle George Magoffin Humphrey — his father's brother — served as the 55th Secretary of the Treasury under President Eisenhower from 1953 to 1957, his signature appearing on approximately 4.5 billion currency bills; and through his mother Katherine Alice Strong, whose father George Hooker Strong traced his line back to Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong, Watts Humphrey had a second independent Hooker-line descent from the same seventeenth-century Connecticut origins. His parents were third cousins — both great-great-grandchildren of Captain James Hooker of Windsor — making Watts Sherman Humphrey the software pioneer one of the most multiply-descended figures in the entire Hooker family tree.

narrative_blocks.header: His son became the father of software quality
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Sherman Humphrey Jr. and his wife Katherine Strong Humphrey — themselves third cousins descended from the same Captain James Hooker of Windsor, Connecticut — had a son, Watts Sherman Humphrey, who became the most influential figure in the history of software engineering: founder of Carnegie Mellon's Software Process Program, architect of the Capability Maturity Model adopted by the Defense Department, and winner of the National Medal of Technology in 2003. His brother George Magoffin Humphrey served simultaneously as Eisenhower's Secretary of the Treasury — making Watts Jr. the connecting figure between two of the most historically significant Hooker-line descendants of the twentieth century, in fields as different as government finance and software engineering.

narrative_blocks.header: She married her own third cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1924 Chicago marriage to Watts Sherman Humphrey Jr. joined two gen-8 Hooker descendants whose common ancestor was Capt. James Hooker, reached through different daughters — a third-cousin union that folded one branch of the family back onto another.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Lawn Cemetery Saginaw per FA — buried in Michigan despite dying in Florida

narrative_blocks.header: Graduated Helmuth College and attended University of Michigan
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline Mary Magoffin Humphrey graduated from Helmuth College in London, Ontario, Canada — an Anglican women's college — before attending the University of Michigan, an unusual educational path for a woman born in Portage, Wisconsin in 1861, at a time when fewer than two percent of American women attended college at all. She married Watts Sherman Humphrey, a Civil War veteran and frontier attorney, in Chicago in January 1888, and their eldest son George Magoffin Humphrey became Eisenhower's Secretary of the Treasury — suggesting that her educational ambition was not lost on the generation she raised.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.photo_notes: Watts Sherman Humphrey Sr. (1844-1916), Civil War veteran and Cheboygan attorney

burial.plot_notes: Forest Lawn Cemetery Saginaw per FA

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War cavalryman, Cheboygan attorney, and director of

education.notes: Studied law under Kilbourne in Lansing before graduating.

career.notes: Corporate counsel for Michigan Sugar and railroads; founded firm after arriving Cheboygan 1869.

career.organization: Mistee Lumber; Phelps Dodge Corporation; mining and smelting interests
career.notes: Significant corporate directorships in industrial Michigan.

military_service.notes: Enlisted December 11, 1863; discharged May 18, 1865. Horse shot under him at Battle of the Wilderness (May 1864); wounded at Battle of Trevilian Station (June 1864). Admitted Harper's Hospital Detroit January 2, 1865.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated University of Michigan Law 1869; went on to found Humphrey & Perks law firm in Cheboygan, Michigan.

narrative_blocks.header: Horse shot at the Wilderness; wounded at Trevilian
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Sherman Humphrey served as a private in Company I, 1st Michigan Cavalry, enlisting December 11, 1863, and saw fierce action: his horse was shot under him at the Battle of the Wilderness in May 1864, and he was wounded at the Battle of Trevilian Station in June 1864 — two of the bloodiest cavalry engagements of the Overland Campaign — before being admitted to Harper's Hospital in Detroit on January 2, 1865. He was honorably discharged May 18, 1865, and afterward studied law under Kilbourne in Lansing before graduating from the University of Michigan in 1869.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded a Cheboygan law firm; ran for Congress
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Sherman Humphrey arrived in Cheboygan, Michigan in 1869 with his University of Michigan law degree and built one of the region's most prominent practices, founding Humphrey & Perks and serving as corporate counsel to Michigan Sugar and railroad interests; he was elected county treasurer in 1880 and in 1890 ran as the Republican candidate for Congress. His corporate reach extended to directorships at Mistee Lumber and Phelps Dodge — one of the nation's largest copper mining companies — making him a significant figure in the industrial development of northern Michigan.

narrative_blocks.header: A corporate lawyer for Michigan's mines and railroads
narrative_blocks.body: From his Cheboygan law practice, Watts Sherman Humphrey built his corporate reach to become a director of Phelps Dodge Corporation — then one of the largest copper mining companies in the United States, with mines in Arizona and New Mexico — and of Mistee Lumber, wiring together the extractive industrial economy of northern Michigan through legal work for railroads and mining interests that made him a significant figure in the region's development. His connections to natural resources and corporate law directly presaged his son George Magoffin Humphrey's career as president of M.A. Hanna Company and ultimately Secretary of the Treasury — two generations of men who ran the industrial sinew of American capitalism before entering public life.

sources.label: Cheboygan History article — The Humphrey Brothers
